FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Release 3.0 baseline data with N=10,691 participants ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen media activity (SMA) as total hours from six SMA activities: watching TV/movies, videos, video games, texting, social networking sites, and video-chatting

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effect models with SMA as predictor and associations tested with externalizing psychopathology, sleep, cognitive measures

FT_IC4.evidence: β = 0.107, P = 0.002 for SMA association; β = 0.117, P = 0.002 for externalizing psychopathology association

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using JIVE approach with ABCD baseline data

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal of Behavioral Addictions 2022, full-length report

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional analysis with SMA as IV and non-SM outcomes (externalizing behavior, brain structural covariation). Includes individual psychological/neural predictors (brain structural covariation patterns).
notes: Brain structural covariation pattern is the latent construct; SMA-specific pathway extractable. Cross-sectional design with psychological predictor (brain measures) means E7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline data with 11,834 children ages 9-10

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures recreational screen time activity as hours of screen-based activities (watching television, video games) per typical weekday and weekend

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects model examining association of general p factor with screen activity; multiple linear regression tests relationship with behavioral measures

FT_IC4.evidence: Coefficient 0.116 with p-value <2.00E-16 for screen activity association with p factor

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) bifactor models with ABCD baseline data

FT_IC6.evidence: 2021 IEEE International Conference on Bioinformatics and Biomedicine (BIBM) conference paper

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is not the sole dependent variable; includes multiple other independent variables and predictors. Model includes individual psychological measures (gender, sleep duration, physical activity, household income). Not longitudinal-only demographic predictor model.
notes: Screen activity measured as composite screen time variable from Youth Screen Time Survey; included as fixed effect in mixed-effects model with other predictors.

FT_IC1.evidence: Discusses and describes ABCD Study, which uses U.S. ABCD data

FT_IC2.evidence: Commentary/resource paper mentions 'screen time monitoring' as a novel technology measure but does not measure a specific smartphone or social media variable in analyses

FT_IC3.evidence: Commentary paper with no original inferential statistical tests linking smartphone/SM variables to outcomes; only descriptive overview of ABCD measures and opportunities

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric results (β, OR, p-values) reported for smartphone/SM associations

FT_IC5.evidence: Commentary/editorial describing the ABCD Study and future research opportunities; no original empirical analysis conducted

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal of Adolescent Health 2021, published commentary article

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable; paper fails FT_IC5 (not original empirical analysis)
notes: This is a special-issue introduction/resource description article without original analysis or reported statistics. Fails on FT_IC3, FT_IC4, and FT_IC5.
